Blog Public Health

The State of Vaccines in the U.S. in 2026

February 25, 2026

Presently, the United States finds itself deeply divided over the direction of vaccine policy. A group of 15 states filed a lawsuit against the Department of Health and Human Services challenging changes to the national childhood vaccine schedule. The lawsuit contends that the updated guidance, which reduced universal recommendations for vaccines such as flu, rotavirus, hepatitis A and B, meningitis, and RSV in favor of “shared decision-making” between doctors and families, undermines long-standing public health practices and puts children at risk. State officials also criticized the dismissal and replacement of all members of the CDC’s Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) by Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. as unlawful and politically driven, a move HHS officials dismissed as a publicity stunt.

The legal battle echoes broader political and scientific disputes over vaccine policy. Federal public health guidance historically shapes state requirements and insurance coverage, but some states are pushing back. For example, the Virginia Department of Health has urged providers to adhere to the American Academy of Pediatrics’ schedule, rejecting the federal changes as unsupported by new data.

Public Opinion and Vaccine Confidence

Amid these policy debates, public sentiment on vaccines has remained broadly supportive. Multiple nationwide polls show strong backing for routine childhood vaccines: about 91 percent of adults believe vaccines are safe for most children, and roughly 79 percent support school requirements for vaccination. This support spans political lines, although confidence in specific aspects of vaccine safety and schedules is not uniform across all groups.

These findings contrast sharply with the political disputes over federal guidance and highlight a disconnect between daily public health practice and high-level policy debates.

The Hepatitis B Vaccination Debate

A particularly contentious issue has been the future of the hepatitis B vaccine, a preventative that has substantially reduced childhood infections since universal newborn vaccination began in 1991. Under Kennedy’s reshaped ACIP, the committee voted to end the routine recommendation that all newborns receive the first hepatitis B dose at birth, instead advising a risk-based approach in which only infants born to mothers with positive or unknown hepatitis B status would be vaccinated at birth. For others, parents are advised to discuss timing with clinicians, potentially delaying the first shot until two months old.

Experts warn that altering the birth dose recommendation risks increasing infection rates, reversing decades of progress. Stat data indicates that hepatitis B vaccination rates among newborns, after rising steadily for years, have begun to slip, with vaccination coverage dropping since 2023 in correlation with the intensified public discourse around vaccines. Some analysts link this trend, in part, to heightened debate following high-profile interviews and messaging from influential figures questioning vaccine practices.

Critics argue that the ACIP’s deliberations lacked robust scientific evidence and underscored a shift away from evidence-based recommendations toward speculation. One committee member later noted that the decision to alter hepatitis B guidance was made without presenting new supporting data.

Federal Leadership on the Line

The vaccine policy debate has reached Senate committees, where President Donald Trump’s nominee for U.S. Surgeon General, Dr. Casey Means, faced rigorous questioning about her support for shared decision-making and her stance on universal vaccination recommendations. Senators from both parties pressed her to affirm widely accepted public health positions, particularly regarding vaccines like hepatitis B, highlighting bipartisan concern over potential weakening of vaccine policy.

What This Means

These developments reflect a broader struggle over how science, public trust, federal authority, and individual choice intersect in vaccination policy. As states and federal agencies clash in court and legislatures, and as public confidence in vaccines persists despite political discord, the trajectory of U.S. immunization programs remains uncertain.

Key Takeaways

  • Policy conflicts have real consequences: Lawsuits by states underscore deep institutional disagreements over federal vaccine guidance and process.

  • Public support for vaccines remains strong: Polling consistently shows wide belief in vaccine safety and support for school requirements, even as policy debates intensify.

  • Hepatitis B policy illustrates stakes: Alterations to long-standing newborn vaccination recommendations could influence infection trends and public trust.

  • Leadership scrutiny continues: Senate hearings highlight bipartisan concerns about balancing scientific evidence with shared decision-making frameworks.
